title: The Rise and Fall of the Great Powers
subtitle: Economic Change and Military Conflict from 1500 to 2000
excerpt: Paul Kennedy's landmark study of the relationship between economic power and military power over five centuries, arguing that great powers decline when they overextend their military commitments beyond their economic base.
keyIdeas: The relative economic power of great powers shifts over time as productivity and technology change
Imperial overstretch — the gap between military commitments and economic resources — drives the decline of great powers
The rise and fall of great powers follows discernible patterns that can be studied historically
keyTakeaways: Great powers rise when their economic base expands faster than their military commitments
Decline begins when military spending crowds out productive investment
The United States in the 1980s showed signs of the same imperial overstretch that doomed earlier powers
No single power has ever remained dominant indefinitely
The key to national power in the modern era is economic productivity, not territorial extent
whoShouldRead: Students of international relations and grand strategy
Anyone interested in why empires rise and fall
Policymakers and strategists thinking about American decline
History readers who enjoy big-picture analysis
whoShouldSkip: Those wanting cultural or intellectual history — this is about power and economics
Readers seeking a simple, deterministic theory of history
People looking for specific policy prescriptions rather than historical analysis
